t: ICD-11 — differs from DIP by 'not fully capturing.'
DSM-5-TR — may be assessed within Other Specified Dissociative Disorder (300.15).
Non-dominant identifications consistently seize dominant control + amnesia.
The phase-oriented approach is adapted for DID (ISSTD 2011). For treatment principles, phases, and specific methodologies, see section 6B60 "Treatment" (including "Treatment methodologies").
Functional recovery with phase-oriented therapy; similar to DIP.
Myth 1: "Partial DID is a 'mild form' of DID"
distinct diagnostic category; functional impairment and trauma burden may be comparable to DID.
Myth 2: "Internal voices are always a psychotic symptom"
dissociative voices differ from psychotic symptoms—internal dialogue, 'own' feeling, weak response to antipsychotics; SCID-D-R for differentiation.
dissociative voices do not respond to antipsychotics; psychotherapy first-line.
Different from factitious disorder (6D50) — no motivation for gain; clinical experience is distressing for the patient.
WHO. ICD-11. 6B61 Partial dissociative identity disorder. 2024.
ISSTD. Guidelines for Treating Dissociative Identity Disorder in Adults, Third Revision. J Trauma Dissociation 2011;12(2):115–187.
Reinders A.A.T.S. et al. J Nerv Ment Dis 2016;204(6):445–457.
